title: The Core of Calvinism, Part 3a: Piety and Prayer
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 7–21-24 - The Core of Calvinism 3a - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Calvinist Drives Toward Piety
subpoints: John Calvin's Institutes, Book 3: "We belong to God — let his wisdom and will rule our actions"
Piety (holiness, Godliness) was inseparable from theology in Calvin's thinking
There is no true knowledge where there is no true piety
The eight-week series theme: living for God's glory with great joy

outline.title: Election Is Unto Obedience
subpoints: 1 Peter 1:1-2: elect according to the foreknowledge of God, in sanctification of the Spirit, unto obedience
Before anything existed, God had already selected people to live unto piety
This obedience flows into hope (1 Peter 1:3), into an incorruptible inheritance (1 Peter 1:4), and into God's keeping power (1 Peter 1:5)
The result: election unto obedience produces holiness of life, daily, now

outline.title: What Is Holiness?
subpoints: Holiness at its most basic means being holy — set apart
Set apart from sin and the world; set apart unto God and his will
Being holy also means being "other" — God transcends creation; the church is to be other than the world
The toothbrush illustration: set apart for one owner's use only

outline.title: What Motivates Piety?
subpoints: Knowing who we are in Christ motivates holiness (1 Peter 1:18-19)
Our identity is not 'sinner' — we are saints, set apart for Christ
It is wrong to identify a Christian by any sin (e.g., 'gay Christian,' 'alcoholic Christian')
Christ does not identify himself with any sin; neither should we define ourselves by sin

outline.title: The Practice of Piety: Prayer
subpoints: Calvin: prayer is the chief exercise of faith
Prayer demonstrates dependence on God, which is an exercise of faith

Prayer was given to us to: fly to God with every need, desire only what is right, receive God's benefits with gratitude, meditate on God's kindness, cultivate delight in God's answers, and confirm God's faithful providence
Calvin's conditions for prayer: heartfelt reverence, heartfelt need and repentance, heartfelt humility and trust, heartfelt confident hope
No is an answer; not now is an answer — God always answers prayer
Holiness also requires repentance, self-denial, cross-bearing, and thinking about the future life

outline.title: What and Whom Should We Pray For?
subpoints: Only for things lawful — in accordance with the revealed will of God in Scripture
For the living and for those yet to be born (Jesus prayed for us in John 17:20-26)
Not for the dead; not for those who have committed the sin unto death (1 John 5:16)
Three unrepentable sins: categorical active denial of Christ (1 John context), blasphemy against the Holy Spirit, apostasy described in Hebrews 6

summary: The third installment of the "Core of Calvinism" series (Part 3a) turns from the doctrines of sovereignty and grace to their necessary fruit: piety. Drawing on John Calvin's Institutes (Book 3, "The Sum of Christian Life"), Pastor Tito Lyro argues that for Calvin — the preeminent Calvinist — theological knowledge and practical piety were inseparable. Turning to 1 Peter 1:1-2, the lesson demonstrates that election is not based on foreseen obedience but is unto obedience — God in eternity past designed a plan to bring people to himself so that they would live holy lives. The sermon defines holiness at its most basic level as being set apart: from sin and the world, and unto God and his will, with the added dimension of being "other" than the rest of humanity. Motivations for piety are rooted in knowing one's identity in Christ (we are saints, not sinners by identity) and in the price paid for redemption. The lesson then focuses extensively on prayer as the chief practice of piety, drawing on Calvin's teaching that prayer is the chief exercise of faith, enumerating the purposes of prayer, and addressing the practical questions of what and whom we should pray for — including a detailed discussion of the sin unto death from 1 John 5:16 and Hebrews 6.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Calvinist Drives Toward Piety

All right, so the rest of us, we're going to have a short version of our third lesson. We're going through a series on Calvinism — not necessarily trying to define the doctrines that have been lately recognized as Calvinism, but looking at how those doctrines are lived out in everyday life. This is the third lesson. It's going to lead to next week. During these eight weeks we're considering the truth that Calvinism translates into daily life as living for God's glory with great joy.

We saw in the first lesson that the marrow of Calvinism, the center of Calvinism, is the sovereignty of God over all things — God is Lord over all things. And then last week we saw that God relates to us by grace, not by any merit on our part. This week, I wanted to talk about piety.

Let me start with a quote from John Calvin himself, from Book 3 of his Institutes. Calvin says: "We belong to God — let us therefore live for him and die for him. We belong to God — let his wisdom and will therefore rule our actions. We belong to God — let all the parts of our life be accordingly strived toward him as our only lawful goal." That's a summary of life, because we belong to God.

One of the things that the Calvinist drives toward is piety. He knows that God is sovereign and that his sovereign grace has redeemed him unto obedience. So piety is very important to him. Now the word piety is not usually part of our vocabulary today. When it is, it's often used as a pejorative — "you're so pious" — as if that's a bad thing. Somebody says that, well, I'll just say, "Why thank you, that's a great compliment." But for centuries, the word piety was equivalent for what we use today: holiness or Godliness.

For Calvin, the preeminent Calvinist, theological understanding and practical piety — truth and usefulness — were inseparable. Theology deals first with knowledge: knowledge of God and of ourselves. But there is no true knowledge where there is no true piety, says Joel Beeke. So if one who claims to be a Christian has all this theological knowledge but that's not translating into piety of life, holiness of life, that knowledge is not really true knowledge. The knowledge of Christ and of Christ's things will lead us to Godliness.

## Election Is Unto Obedience

If you have a Bible, turn to 1 Peter chapter 1. I think Peter here helps us understand this idea of the importance of piety in the context of the Christian life. What Peter shows us is that God, in eternity past, before he created anything, chose people for himself to bring through Jesus Christ for one particular purpose — and that particular purpose was obedience, holiness, piety. Even before anything existed, God in his mind had already selected people to live unto piety, unto Godliness.

Look at verse 1 and 2: "Peter, an Apostle of Jesus Christ, to the pilgrims of the Dispersion in Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia, elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, in sanctification of the Spirit" — and then you have the word "unto" — "for obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ."

Peter says that we are elected unto obedience — not because of obedience, but unto obedience. In eternity past, God designed this plan in which he is going to bring people to himself through Jesus Christ so that they can be holy, so that they can obey him, so that they can be pious in the way they live.

And that obedience flows into hope because we are also elected unto hope in verse 3: "Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who according to His abundant mercy has begotten us again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead." And then unto an incorruptible inheritance in verse 4: "to an inheritance incorruptible and undefiled, and that does not fade away, reserved in heaven for you." And then unto the keeping power of God in verse 5: "who are kept by the power of God through faith for salvation ready to be revealed in the last time."

And because of that, verse 13 onward tells us: "Therefore, gird up the loins of your mind... be holy, for I am holy." Election unto obedience results in holiness of life — now. That's how the doctrine affects the way we live, every day, piety of life, holiness, Godliness — now.

## What Is Holiness?

So what is piety? What is holiness? At the most basic level, holiness means being holy. And being holy means being set apart — set apart from something, set apart unto something. Being holy means being set apart from sin and the world, and set apart unto God and his will.

The other side of holiness is being "other." When you say that God is holy, you are not only saying that God is perfect without sin, but also that God is other than this world — he transcends creation, he is not part of creation. And when it says to us, "Be holy for I am holy," it means that we are other than the rest of the people of the world. We are set apart, other than — the church is different than the world.

That's why it's so important that the church doesn't try to be like the world. There's no power in that. If we get our ideas of management from the world, if we get ideas on how to attract people from the world, if we get ideas on what should be preached from the world, we just cease to have a reason to exist. The church is not very good at mimicking the world, and the world is going to do a better job at being the world.

An illustration that helps us understand holiness well is a toothbrush. Most people are very particular about their toothbrush. You set it apart for your own use. You don't want other people using that toothbrush. The Holy Spirit has set us apart for his use only — and if we are used for something else, that is a really bad use.

## What Motivates Piety?

So what motivates holiness, the Bible gives us? Peter here in verse 18 says: "Knowing that you were not redeemed with corruptible things, like silver or gold, from your aimless conduct received by tradition from your fathers, but with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot."

Peter says, what's the motivation to be holy? You know Christ. Because you know Christ, you can be holy. And it's interesting that he writes this idea of knowing as something that we have known and continue to know. Really the motivation for holiness is for you to have a clear picture of your identity. Who are you? The Bible says that you are a saint — which means one set apart for Christ. That's what a Christian is.

Your identity as a follower of Christ is not "sinner." That's important. A sinner is one who can only sin. That's not you. In Christ he gives you the grace that you need to resist. We still sin, but sin is not what identifies us — Christ is.

That's why it's so wrong to say that you are a "gay Christian" or an "alcoholic Christian" — because you cannot identify a Christian by any sin. Christ does not identify himself with any sin. You can be a Christian who struggles with the allurement of substances, but you cannot be an alcoholic forever and be a Christian. The way we think of ourselves is very important as we live pious and holy lives.

## The Practice of Piety: Prayer

Remember how I started by talking about the Institutes of the Christian Religion by John Calvin, spanning two volumes. The largest chapter in the whole book — the second-longest — is the chapter on prayer. That chapter is often published by itself because it is so devotional.

Calvin helps us understand the practice of piety when he writes that one of the basic things about holiness is prayer. A Christian who is living a pious life is a Christian who prays. He calls prayer "the chief exercise of faith." Does it make sense that prayer is the chief exercise of faith? It's not witnessing, it's not evangelism, but prayer being the chief exercise of faith. Why? Because prayer shows dependence on God, and that is an exercise of faith.

He says that prayer was given to us so that:
- We can fly to God with every need and gain from him what is lacking in ourselves to live the Christian life
- We can learn to desire wholeheartedly only what is right as we place all our petitions before God
- We can become prepared to receive God's benefits with humble gratitude
- We can be led to meditate on God's kindness as we receive what we have asked for
- We can cultivate the proper spirit of delight for God's answers in prayer
- We can confirm God's faithful providence

And that's why our prayer should not be only petitions, but also praises and adoration. Because in those we recognize what God has done for us and we grow in thankfulness.

You might say, God doesn't answer my prayers — I prayed for this and that and it's not happening. Brothers and sisters, "no" is an answer. "Not now" is an answer. God always answers our prayers. Sometimes it feels like he's not there. But we have to trust that, whatever is going on, this is the perfect will of God for our lives.

Calvin says that when we pray we should have a heartfelt sense of reverence — we're not praying to our buddy, we're not having a talk with a friend, we're praying to the Almighty God, Creator of heaven and earth, Savior of the world. We pray with a heartfelt sense of need and repentance. We pray with a heartfelt sense of humility and trust in God. And we pray with a heartfelt sense of confident hope.

Holiness also requires repentance, self-denial, cross-bearing, and thinking about the future life in the present life.

## What and Whom Should We Pray For?

What should we pray for? Only for things that are lawful — only for things in the will of God as revealed in Scripture, not his hidden will. We don't pray for things against what the Bible says. For example, we don't pray, "Lord, give me wisdom whether I should marry an unbeliever." God already answered that in his Word.

We are to pray for the living — those who are alive. We are to pray for those yet to be born. We see that Jesus himself does this in John 17: in verses 1-5 he prays for himself, in verses 6-19 he prays for his disciples, and in verses 20-26 he prays for you — who at his time had not yet been born.

But it is not appropriate to pray for the dead, and we are not to pray for those who have committed the sin unto death. 1 John 5:16 says, "Do not pray for those who have committed the sin unto death." There are three categories of sins that are unrepentable — and therefore there is no point in praying for those people.

The sin unto death in the context of 1 John seems to be categorically, actively, and in a crusading way denying that Christ is who he says he is — having first confessed faith in him and then denying that. There is also the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit, which seems to be attributing to Satan the work of the Holy Spirit. And Hebrews 6 talks about those who have tasted the gifts of grace and what seems to be a credible faith, then apostatizing — with no possibility of repentance.

So I hope you understand that now we're going to have to go to nine weeks instead of eight, because in God's good providence we heard the good reports from the Vanderhuffs and from Ellie today. So let's pray. Father, thank you that you are good. Even in times of suffering, we thank you that you are present with us in prayer, even in times of suffering. Pray to help us to be pious people, people who have been set apart because of Christ. In his name, amen.
